title: JWT (JSON Web Tokens)
objectives: Understand JWT via a real-life analogy
See the three parts: Header, Payload, Signature
Distinguish signing algorithms (HS256 symmetric vs RS256 asymmetric)
Handle expiry, refresh tokens, and revocation properly

sections.markdown:
**Think of a JWT as a tamper-proof concert wristband.**

- When you buy a ticket at the box office (log in), the venue gives you a WRISTBAND. The wristband HAS your name, section, seat number written on it in plain sight (the PAYLOAD). It also has a HOLOGRAM that only the venue can produce (the SIGNATURE).
- Every time you approach a checkpoint (an API endpoint), you flash your wristband. The checkpoint sees your name + section AND checks the hologram — 'yes, this was issued by us, and nobody has tampered with it'. **The checkpoint doesn't need to phone the box office** to verify. All the info + proof-of-authenticity is on the wristband itself.
- If someone forges the payload (changes 'section G' to 'section VIP'), the hologram no longer matches — instantly rejected.
- Wristbands **expire** at midnight (`exp` claim). After that, the checkpoint refuses even a valid-signed wristband. If you want to stay longer, you need a NEW wristband (refresh token).

**In code:** a JWT is a compact, URL-safe string carrying user identity + claims, cryptographically signed so nobody can forge or tamper with it. **Stateless auth's superpower:** the server doesn't need to look anything up. Verify the signature → trust the payload → you're done. This scales to millions of requests without hitting the auth database.

**Trade-offs:** revoking a JWT before it expires is HARD (you'd need a blacklist, which defeats statelessness). Sensitive data in the payload is only signed, NOT encrypted — anyone can decode it. Short expiry + refresh tokens is the standard workaround.

**A JWT (JSON Web Token)** is a compact, URL-safe way to represent CLAIMS between two parties (RFC 7519). Format: **`header.payload.signature`** — three base64url-encoded parts joined by dots. The header names the signing algorithm; the payload holds the claims (subject, issuer, expiry, custom fields); the signature is a HMAC or asymmetric signature over `header.payload` using a secret or private key. The server issuing the token signs; anyone with the public key (or shared secret) can VERIFY. Statelessness comes from carrying identity IN the token — no database lookup per request.
## The three parts — decoded

sections.code:
A real JWT looks like this (single line, no spaces):

  eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJzdWIiOiI0MiIsIm5hbWUiOiJBZGEiLCJleHAiOjE3MDAwMDAwMDB9.
  hZaBBnO7iJx6yAsc3-uLxrK3zXWzX8SB7YvHVwCEuBM

Join with '.', split into three parts, base64url-decode each:

1. HEADER (JSON — algorithm + type):
   { "alg": "HS256", "typ": "JWT" }

2. PAYLOAD (JSON — the CLAIMS):
   { "sub": "42", "name": "Ada", "exp": 1700000000, "iat": 1699996400,
     "iss": "https://auth.example.com", "aud": "api.example.com",
     "scope": "read write" }

3. SIGNATURE (binary — HMAC or RSA output):
   HMAC_SHA256(base64(header) + '.' + base64(payload), SECRET)

The payload is READABLE by anyone. It's just SIGNED, not ENCRYPTED.
Sensitive data (SSN, cards) does NOT belong in a JWT payload.

sections.markdown: ## Standard claims (RFC 7519)

rows: Who is the token intended for?
Unix timestamp when the token expires
Unique token identifier for revocation lists

sections.markdown: **The interview one-liner:** always verify `iss`, `aud`, `exp` on EVERY request. Missing any check = tokens issued by other services / for other services / that expired get accepted. This is the #1 real-world JWT bug.
## Signing algorithms — HS256 vs RS256 (the big choice)

rows: Auth server issues; many services verify
JWKS endpoint publishes public keys automatically
Easy — publish new key in JWKS, old ones expire

sections.markdown: **The rule:** use **RS256** when the token is verified by SERVICES OTHER than the one that issued it (microservices, third-party SDKs, mobile). Use **HS256** ONLY when the same service does both. Getting this wrong lets any resource server impersonate the auth server (HS256 attack).
## Sample program — issue + verify a JWT in Java
## Access token + refresh token — the standard pattern

sections.code:
Two tokens per session:

  ACCESS TOKEN — short-lived (10-15 min), sent on every API request.
     If it leaks, damage is bounded to the expiry window.
     Stored in memory ideally; sometimes in HttpOnly cookie or Secure storage.

  REFRESH TOKEN — long-lived (7-30 days), sent ONLY to /auth/refresh.
     Trades in for a new access token. Stored in Secure storage / HttpOnly cookie.
     Rotated on each use (each refresh returns a NEW refresh token, invalidates the old one).
     Bound to a device/session for detection of theft.

Flow:

  1. User logs in (POST /auth/login with credentials).
  2. Server returns { accessToken, refreshToken, expiresIn }.
  3. Client uses accessToken for API calls.
  4. accessToken expires (401 from API).
  5. Client sends refreshToken to POST /auth/refresh.
  6. Server verifies refreshToken, issues new access + refresh tokens.
  7. If refresh token is REUSED (already invalidated), server suspects theft →
     invalidates ALL sessions for that user → forces relogin. This is 'refresh
     token rotation' — Auth0/Okta/Keycloak all do this.

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Not verifying `aud` and `iss`.** Someone else's valid JWT is accepted. Always check both.
**Mistake 2: Long-lived access tokens (24h+).** If it leaks, attacker has a day of access. Fix: 10-15 min access + refresh tokens for renewal.
**Mistake 3: Putting secrets in the payload.** JWT payload is base64-encoded, NOT encrypted. Anyone with the token can decode. Sensitive data → server DB, referenced by `sub`.
**Mistake 4: `alg: none` acceptance.** Some libraries used to accept unsigned tokens if header said `alg: none`. Always REJECT `none`. Use libraries with allowlists of accepted algorithms.
**Mistake 5: HS256 with a weak secret.** Under 32 bytes → brute-forceable. Use 256+ bit random secret. Better: RS256 with proper key management.
**Mistake 6: HS256 across services.** Every service needs the secret → any service that holds the secret can FORGE tokens. Use RS256 for multi-service.
**Mistake 7: Trying to revoke a JWT.** By design you can't easily. Use short expiry + refresh-token rotation instead; or accept a small delay before revocation takes effect.
## Best practices
**1. Short-lived access tokens (10-15 min).** Long-lived refresh tokens (7-30 days) that ROTATE on each use.
**2. RS256 (asymmetric)** for multi-service; HS256 only within one service.
**3. Always verify `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, `nbf`, signature — all of them.** Use a mature library; don't roll your own.
**4. Store on client:** access token in memory (or HttpOnly cookie); refresh token in Secure Storage / HttpOnly SameSite=Strict cookie. NEVER in localStorage (XSS steals it).
**5. Refresh-token rotation with reuse detection.** Detect stolen tokens by 'this token was already exchanged; someone else has it'.
**6. Include only necessary claims.** Keep tokens small — they go on every request.
**7. JWKS endpoint** for public-key distribution when using RS256. Cache with proper TTL.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Decode a token.** Paste one into jwt.io; identify header, payload, signature. Change a payload field; see the signature mismatch.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Issue + verify HS256.** Java or Node — one script that issues + one that verifies. Cover the failure paths: wrong secret, expired, wrong audience.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — RS256 with keypair.** Generate RSA keypair; sign with private; verify with public. Publish public via a static endpoint.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Access + refresh flow.** POST /auth/login returns both. POST /auth/refresh trades old for new. Detect refresh-token reuse and revoke session.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — JWKS + Spring Resource Server.** Publish JWKS; consume in a second Spring Boot service.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Attack simulations.** Try `alg: none`; try modifying a claim; try using an expired token 3 seconds after expiry (leeway); assert your server rejects each.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is a JWT and what does it look like?**

**A:** A JWT (JSON Web Token, RFC 7519) is a compact, URL-safe token carrying CLAIMS (user identity + metadata) signed cryptographically so it can't be forged or tampered with. Format: three base64url-encoded parts joined by dots — `header.payload.signature`. The **header** names the algorithm (`HS256`, `RS256`). The **payload** holds claims — standard ones like `sub` (subject/user), `iss` (issuer), `exp` (expiry), plus custom fields like `scope`. The **signature** is a HMAC or RSA signature over `header.payload`. Anyone can decode the payload; only the signing key holder can produce a valid signature. Enables stateless auth — servers verify without a database lookup.

**Q2: HS256 vs RS256 — when to use which?**

**A:** **HS256** — HMAC with SHA-256. **Symmetric** — same secret signs and verifies. Fast, small. Best when the ISSUER and VERIFIER are the same service (single monolith). **RS256** — RSA with SHA-256. **Asymmetric** — private key signs; public key verifies. Slower, larger tokens. Best when the issuer is separate from many verifiers (microservices, mobile apps, third-party integrations). **The rule:** if any service other than the issuer needs to verify, use RS256. Otherwise any verifier could sign fake tokens with the shared secret. RS256's public key can be published via **JWKS** (a JSON endpoint listing keys with IDs) — consumers cache and refresh.

**Q3: How do you revoke a JWT?**

**A:** You mostly can't — that's the trade-off of statelessness. Since verification doesn't hit a database, adding 'is this token revoked?' would defeat the whole point. **Two approaches:** **(1) Short-lived access tokens + long-lived refresh tokens.** Access tokens live 10-15 min; revocation waits for expiry. Refresh tokens live longer but are single-use (rotate on each refresh); server can invalidate the refresh chain server-side. This is the STANDARD pattern (Auth0, Okta, Keycloak). **(2) Blacklist by `jti`** — server keeps a list of revoked JWT IDs (typically in Redis with TTL matching token expiry). Small state, but adds a lookup per request — pragmatic middle-ground. **Rule:** use approach 1 by default; approach 2 only when immediate revocation matters (fraud incident, admin lockout).

**Q4: What is a refresh token and why do you need one?**

**A:** A refresh token is a LONG-LIVED (7-30 days) credential used SOLELY to obtain new access tokens from `/auth/refresh`. Never sent to regular API endpoints. **Why:** access tokens should be short-lived (10-15 min) so leaks are bounded, but constantly asking the user to re-log-in is bad UX. The refresh token bridges the gap: the client silently exchanges the refresh for a new access + refresh pair. **Best practice: refresh-token rotation** — each refresh returns a NEW refresh token, invalidating the old. If the old one is reused (attacker has it), the server can DETECT theft and force re-login. Store refresh tokens carefully: HttpOnly SameSite=Strict cookie or Secure Storage; NEVER localStorage (XSS).

**Q5: What claims MUST you verify on every request?**

**A:** **Signature** — the whole point; if it's wrong, the token was forged. **`exp` (expiry)** — reject expired tokens with small leeway (30-60 seconds for clock skew). **`iss` (issuer)** — must match your expected auth server. Prevents accepting tokens from other issuers. **`aud` (audience)** — must include your service's ID. Prevents tokens issued for another service being replayed against yours. **`nbf` (not before)** — reject if issued in the future (unlikely but possible). **Optional but recommended:** verify the `alg` header is on your allowlist (`HS256` or `RS256`, NOT `none`). Missing any of these checks is a real vulnerability. Every mature library does this for you — just configure it correctly.

**Q6: Where should the client store a JWT?**

**A:** **Never in localStorage** — any XSS on your site can read localStorage and exfiltrate the token. Instant hijack. **Options in order of security:** **(1) HttpOnly cookie with SameSite=Strict, Secure flag** — browser auto-attaches to same-origin requests; JS can't read it. Needs CSRF protection because cookies auto-send. Best for browser web apps. **(2) In-memory only** — token lives in a JS variable; lost on reload. Silent refresh handles reload. Immune to XSS reading (but not to XSS injecting requests). **(3) Native Secure Storage** — Android Keystore / iOS Keychain / Flutter `flutter_secure_storage`. For mobile apps. **(4) Session storage** — slightly better than localStorage (per-tab, cleared on close), but still readable by XSS. **Rule for mobile:** always Secure Storage. **Rule for web:** HttpOnly cookie + CSRF token, or in-memory + silent refresh.

**Q7: What's the `alg: none` attack?**

**A:** A historical vulnerability: JWTs have an `alg` field in the header. Some early libraries would ACCEPT tokens with `alg: none` (no signature). An attacker could take any legit-looking JWT, change the payload, set `alg: none`, drop the signature → server accepted it. **Fix:** libraries now require an EXPLICIT allowlist of accepted algorithms. **Modern rule:** always specify the expected algorithm; NEVER trust `header.alg` alone. `JWT.require(Algorithm.HMAC256(secret))` — only HS256 with THIS secret is accepted. Any other algorithm or unsigned token → rejected. **Related attack:** algorithm confusion — issue a token signed with HS256 using the PUBLIC key of an RS256 keypair as the HMAC secret. If the server uses the public key as the HS256 secret, it verifies! Fix: always specify the expected algorithm explicitly.

## Real-world usage

- **Every OAuth2 access token / OIDC id-token** is a JWT (usually RS256).
- **Auth0, Okta, Keycloak, AWS Cognito** — all issue JWTs.
- **Firebase Auth** — id-tokens are JWTs.
- **Spring Security's OAuth2 Resource Server** — full JWT verification out of the box.
- **AWS API Gateway JWT authorizers** — JWT verification at the edge.
- **Kubernetes ServiceAccount tokens** — JWTs signed by the cluster.
- **Never:** JWTs for session cookies on a monolith — regular sessions are simpler and revocable.

## Advanced notes

- **JWE (encrypted JWT)** — encrypts the payload. For truly-sensitive claims. Rare; usually you just don't put sensitive data in tokens.
- **JWKS refresh** — libraries cache the JWKS response with a TTL; refresh when a `kid` (key ID) isn't found.
- **Key rotation** — publish new key with new `kid` in JWKS; issue new tokens with new kid; old kid still verifies old tokens until they expire.
- **`kid` (key ID) header claim** — tells verifiers which JWKS key to use.
- **`nbf` (not before)** — for tokens that shouldn't be valid until a specific time.
- **Custom claims** — namespace your claims (`https://myapp.com/roles`) to avoid conflicts with standards.
- **Introspection endpoint** — OAuth2 servers can be asked 'is this token active?'; enables server-side revocation at cost of a round trip.
- **DPoP (Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession)** — bind a token to a specific client key; makes stolen tokens useless.
- **PASETO** — an alternative to JWT with fewer footguns; consider for new designs.
- **`Bearer` token spec (RFC 6750)** — `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.
- **OIDC id_token vs OAuth2 access_token** — id_token proves identity to the client; access_token authorizes API calls.

flashcards.back: header.payload.signature — three base64url parts joined by dots.
NO — only base64 encoded. Anyone can read. Only signed for integrity.
HS256 shared secret (single service). RS256 private/public keypair (multi-service). Prefer RS256 for microservices.
Signature + exp + iss + aud + nbf. Missing any = vulnerability.
Hard by design. Use short access tokens (10-15 min) + rotating refresh tokens (7-30 days). Blacklist by jti if needed.
Mobile: Secure Storage. Web: HttpOnly cookie or in-memory. NEVER localStorage.

quiz.explain: Readable by anyone with the token.
Public key verifies without leaking signing capability.

options: Hard — use short expiry + refresh rotation, or jti blacklist
HttpOnly cookie or in-memory (NEVER localStorage due to XSS)

challenges.text: Paste a JWT into jwt.io; identify sub, exp, iss, aud.
Change one character in the payload; see the signature no longer match.
Issue + verify HS256 token in Java or Node; cover expired + wrong-audience failures.
Generate RSA keypair; sign with private; verify with public.
Access + refresh flow with rotation; detect reused refresh token.
Publish a JWKS endpoint; consume in Spring Boot Resource Server.
Simulate attacks: alg: none, modified payload, expired token; assert all rejected.
